text: The patient is positioned supine on the operating table with a bump placed under the right side of the body to elevate it, preparing for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon makes a 5mm incision in the right anterior chest at the sixth intercostal space.
Under direct visualization, the surgeon places two additional 5mm ports in the mid axillary line, enhancing access for the procedure.
The surgeon observes a mass in the mediastinum bulging into the right chest, with mammary vessels visible anterior to the mass and the phrenic nerve positioned posteriorly.

The surgeon identifies key anatomical structures in the mediastinum, including the azygos vein, superior intercostal vein branching from the azygos vein, intercostal veins, superior vena cava, phrenic nerve, and mammary vessels.
The surgeon dissects a plane between the visceral pleura of the medial aspect of the right lung and the pleura overlying the mediastinal mass, carefully separating these tissue layers.
The surgeon uses hook cautery to perform anterior dissection between the mass and the sternum, extending the dissection into the left pleural space.
The surgeon observes that the mass is fixed superiorly and places a fourth port in the midaxillary line to aid in the dissection process.

The surgeon uses a combination of ligature and blunt dissection with an endokitner to carefully separate the mass from the innominate vein, ensuring clear visualization of the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon applies significant traction to mobilize the mass extending into the neck, carefully separating it from the innominate vein and the aorta.
The surgeon uses the endokitner to carefully dissect the portion of the mass that is adherent to the pericardium, separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon retracts the mass posteriorly after freeing it cranially and caudally, allowing for mobilization of the left lateral aspect of the mass away from the pleura of the left lung.
An additional trocar is placed in the left chest at the fifth intercostal space in the midaxillary line due to the inability to identify the left phrenic nerve from the right chest.
The surgeon visualizes the intact left phrenic nerve, confirming its preservation. The mass is then mobilized into the right chest and removed from the mediastinum.
The mediastinum is observed to be hemostatic, with no active bleeding, and the pericardium appears intact after the dissection.
The surgeon enlarges the middle port site in the right midaxillary line to facilitate the exteriorization of the mass, which is placed into a fifteen millimeter endocutch bag for removal.
The surgeon irrigates the right chest cavity and places a 19 French Blake drain through the inferior site into the mediastinal space, ensuring proper drainage.
